text: In this video, the authors present a pure transanal laparoscopic repair of early rectovaginal fistula in a seventy-four year old lady submitted three weeks before to a laparoscopic anterior resection of the rectum with M-block hysterectomy for a rectal adenocarcinoma.
The surgeon inserts a reusable D-port into the anus and utilizes curved reusable instruments for the procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by performing a lavage of the cavity, followed by an exploration of the lower rectum using surgical instruments.
The video shows a large defect in the lower rectum, extending from the twelve to three o'clock position, visibly connecting the rectum to the vagina.
The surgeon inserts a Vicryl 2-0 suture through the central port orifice, preparing to secure tissue within the surgical field.

A monocurve reusable needle holder is introduced through the port orifice at the three o'clock position, while monocurve grasping forceps are inserted through the port orifice at the nine o'clock position.
The surgeon uses figure-of-eight sutures to close the large defect located between the lower rectum and the vagina, visible from twelve to three o'clock positions.
The surgeon passes the suture through the inferior edge and then the superior edge of the large defect, which communicates between the lower rectum and the vagina.

The surgeon estimates the large defect to be two centimeters in size and located twelve centimeters away from the anal margin, visually assessing its position relative to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon employs an intraluminal knotting technique to secure the Vicryl-two-zero suture within the surgical site.
The surgeon worked in ergonomic conditions without clashing of the instrument's tips and without conflict of the surgeon's hands thanks to the curves of the instruments.
The surgeon uses monocurved scissors to cut the suture, which is then removed through the port orifice located at the nine o'clock position.
The surgeon places additional sutures using the intraluminal knotting technique, securing them at the inferior and superior edges of the defect.
The surgeon inspects the closure defects and identifies an improperly closed superior edge, prompting the placement of an additional suture to ensure proper closure.
The surgeon continues to perform intraluminal knotting, maintaining ergonomic positioning to avoid instrument tip clashing and hand conflicts.
The surgeon performs a final injection of methylene blue through the vaginal route, observing no passage of the dye into the lower rectum, indicating successful closure of the surgical site.
Consequently, the procedure was finished at that point.
Due to the reusable nature of the implemented material, no additional costs were required.
After two months, the gastrographin enema showed a complete healing of the fistula, hence the protective ileostomy was closed.
